flashcards.front: What is gravitation?
flashcards.back: The force of attraction between any two objects in the universe.

flashcards.front: State the Universal Law of Gravitation.
flashcards.back: Every object attracts every other object with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

flashcards.front: Write the formula for gravitational force.
flashcards.back: F = G × (M × m) / d², where G is the universal gravitational constant.

flashcards.front: What is the value of the universal gravitational constant (G)?
flashcards.back: 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².

flashcards.front: What is free fall?
flashcards.back: The motion of an object towards the earth under the influence of gravitational force alone.

flashcards.front: What is acceleration due to gravity?
flashcards.back: The acceleration experienced by an object in free fall due to earth’s gravitational force, denoted by g.

flashcards.front: What is the average value of g on earth?
Differentiate between mass and weight.
flashcards.back: Mass is the measure of inertia and remains constant everywhere; weight is the force of gravity acting on mass and varies with location.

flashcards.front: What is the relation between weight and mass?
flashcards.back: W = m × g.

flashcards.front: What is the weight of an object on the moon compared to earth?
flashcards.back: Weight on the moon is 1/6th of its weight on earth.

flashcards.front: Define thrust.
flashcards.back: The force acting on a surface in a direction perpendicular to it.

flashcards.front: Define pressure.
flashcards.back: Pressure = Thrust / Area; SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

flashcards.front: What is buoyancy?
flashcards.back: The upward force exerted by a fluid on an object immersed in it.

flashcards.front: State Archimedes’ Principle.
flashcards.back: A body immersed fully or partially in a fluid experiences an upward force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by it.

flashcards.front: Define density.
flashcards.back: Density = Mass / Volume.

flashcards.front: Why does the earth not move towards a falling apple?
flashcards.back: Because the earth’s mass is extremely large compared to the apple, so its acceleration is negligible.

flashcards.front: Why do all objects fall at the same rate in vacuum?
flashcards.back: Because acceleration due to gravity is independent of mass.

flashcards.front: Why does a sheet of paper fall slower than a stone?
flashcards.back: Because air resistance opposes the motion of the paper more than the stone.

flashcards.front: Why do camels run easily in deserts?
flashcards.back: Because their broad feet spread the thrust over a larger area, reducing pressure on sand.

flashcards.front: Why do knives have sharp edges?
flashcards.back: To concentrate thrust on a smaller area, increasing pressure for cutting.

flashcards.front: Why does a cork float while an iron nail sinks?
flashcards.back: Because cork has lower density than water, while iron has higher density than water.

flashcards.front: Why do ships made of iron float but iron sheets sink?
flashcards.back: Because ships displace more water, creating buoyant force greater than their weight, while sheets do not.

flashcards.front: Why do athletes land on sand or cushioned beds in high jump?
flashcards.back: To increase the time of impact, reducing force by lowering the rate of change of momentum.
